conversation.message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.

It sounds like past attempts at quitting have left you feeling hopeless about trying again, and 20 years of smoking has made it feel like a habit you're not sure you can break. What did you think about your previous attempts that might have worked a little, even if they didn't stick?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

It sounds like you've been quite hard on yourself about your weight, and that's really affecting your motivation to quit smoking. When you're feeling stressed about your weight, what's often happening in the moment that makes you reach for a cigarette?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about quitting smoking, and past attempts at dieting haven't gone well for you. Can you tell me more about what happens when you're not in the right mindset - are there specific situations or emotions that tend to make you want to smoke?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?

You're not making any promises, and that's okay. You're still considering the possibility of quitting, which is a big step in itself. Can you tell me more about what you usually do when you feel stressed or anxious, as those might be situations that often lead to smoking for you?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit resistant to the idea of quitting, and it's okay to feel that way. Can you tell me more about what's making you feel like you're not ready to quit smoking right now?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

You sound frustrated with previous attempts to quit, and it's like smoking has become a part of your identity. What does a typical day look like for you, and are there any specific situations or emotions that make you reach for a cigarette?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

I can understand why you'd feel that way, given your past experiences. You mentioned feeling anxious and depressed when trying to quit, but can you tell me more about what's happening in those moments, like what's triggering those feelings?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

It sounds like you feel misunderstood by your doctor and your loved ones, and that makes it harder for you to feel motivated to quit. Can you tell me more about what you think would make them understand and support you better in your quit journey?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.
I can understand why you'd feel that way, given all the times you've tried to quit before. What is it about your previous attempts that makes you think you won't be able to do it this time?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain and hesitant, but at the same time, you're willing to take a small step towards quitting. What do you think is the biggest challenge for you right now in even thinking about reducing your smoking?

Honestly, I'm feeling really frustrated. I've been trying to quit for weeks now, but it's just not sticking. I've been smoking for about 10 years, and I know it's not good for me, but it's hard to stop.
I can sense your frustration, and it's completely normal to feel that way when trying to quit. What specific situations or events seem to make you reach for a cigarette when you're trying to quit?

Well, I've been feeling really guilty about smoking around my niece and nephew. They're always asking me why I smell like smoke, and it breaks my heart. I want to be a good role model for them, and I don't want to die young from some smoking-related illness.

You're really motivated to quit for their sake, and that's beautiful. Can you think of a small reward you could give yourself the first week after quitting, something that would help you feel proud and motivated to keep going?
Hmm...I'd say about a 6? I mean, I've tried before and failed, so I'm not sure if I can really do it this time.
Rachel, it sounds like those past attempts to quit have been weighing on you a bit. What did you find that worked a little bit, even if it was just for a day or two, in those attempts?

I think I need more support. My friends and family are all smokers, so it's hard to stay motivated when everyone around me is still smoking. And as a freelancer, I work from home, so I don't have coworkers to support me either.

That makes sense about needing support, Rachel. Have you considered sharing your quit plan with a few close friends or family members and asking them to hold you accountable and support you in your decision to quit?
Yeah, I've thought about it, but I don't know...I'm not really sure where to start or if it would even help.

It sounds like you're hesitant about seeking additional support, Rachel. Would you be open to trying a simpler approach, like keeping a journal to track your triggers and cravings, to see if that helps you identify any patterns and find ways to cope with them?
Yeah, okay. I guess it couldn't hurt to try.
Now that you're open to seeking support, Rachel, I'd like to suggest that you start by calling or attending that support group meeting to see if it feels like a good fit for you.

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit a couple of times before. It didn't work out. I guess I'm just worried about the cost of quitting. I've heard those prescription medications can be really expensive.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain and worried about the cost of quitting, which makes you feel hesitant about giving up smoking. Can you think of any specific situations or emotions that make you more likely to reach for a cigarette?

Well, like I said, the cost is a big deal. I'm not sure if my insurance will cover the medications or the nicotine replacement therapy. And even if it does, I'm not sure if I can afford the copays. And then there's the fear of failure. I've tried to quit before, and I didn't make it. I don't want to disappoint my family and friends again. As for my smoking habits, I usually smoke about a pack a day.

I can see that the cost and fear of failure are weighing on you a lot. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about disappointing your loved ones - is it the fear of letting them down, or something else?
Okay, that makes sense. I didn't realize my nicotine dependence level was that high. And yeah, let's talk about those options. I want to know what might work best for me.

I'm glad we're exploring the options for you. You mentioned that the cost is a big deal, but it seems like there's also a deeper concern about failing to quit again. Can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you think about quitting and potentially failing?
Okay, that sounds good. I think I'd like to try the nicotine replacement therapy. And what about counseling and support groups? How do those work?

You're considering nicotine replacement therapy, which is a great first step. When you're not actively working on quitting, are there any situations or emotions that tend to trigger you to smoke, like stress, certain social situations, or even just having a cup of coffee?
Yeah, I knew it was bad for me, but I didn't realize it was that bad.
You're acknowledging the reality of the risks associated with smoking. Can you tell me, what usually triggers your desire to smoke when you're trying to quit?
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan. I can do that. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I can actually do this this time.

You feel more hopeful about quitting this time, which is a big step. What are some of the situations or emotions that make it feel really hard for you to stick to quitting, like when you're with friends or when you're feeling stressed?
Honestly, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to quit smoking. It's not a big deal. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I'm fine.

It sounds like you're feeling pretty confident about your smoking habit, and it's not a big concern for you right now. Can you tell me what would make you consider that maybe quitting smoking isn't a bad idea after all?
Quitting? Nah, I don't think so. I've tried it before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to gain weight, and I don't want to give up the social aspect of smoking. It's part of who I am, you know?

It sounds like the social aspect of smoking is really important to you, and that's part of why you're hesitant to quit. Can you tell me more about what happens when you're with friends or in social situations and everyone is smoking?
I don't know...I've seen friends who quit and they just ballooned up. I don't want that to happen to me. And what about when I'm out with friends at the bar? Smoking is just part of the experience.

You're worried about gaining weight and feeling left out when you're with friends at the bar. Would you be willing to try politely saying no to cigarettes when offered, and remind yourself why you decided to quit, just to see how it feels?
It's just...I'm a bartender, and smoking is part of the culture. My friends all smoke, and it's just what we do. If I quit, I'd feel like I'm missing out or something.

It sounds like being a bartender and surrounded by smoking has become a big part of your identity, and you're worried about losing that connection with your friends. Can you think of a time when you went out with your friends at the bar without smoking, and how that experience was for you?
I guess so...but I'm still not sure I want to quit.

You're still unsure about quitting, but you're willing to consider some alternatives. Can you think of any specific moments or situations when you've managed to cut back on smoking or resist the urge to smoke, even if it was just for a little while?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit before, but I started again. I don't know why I can't just stay quit. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.
It sounds like you've been in this cycle before and it's frustrating for you. How long have you been smoking at the current rate of starting and quitting again?

Well, I was doing okay for a while. I had cut down to just a few cigarettes a day, and I was feeling pretty proud of myself. But then I started getting really stressed with work, and I just fell back into old habits. I'd take a break, and the next thing I knew, I was smoking again. It's like my brain just automatically goes to cigarettes when I'm feeling overwhelmed.

It sounds like stress at work threw you off track, and you fell back into smoking again. What did you miss about smoking when you were doing well and cutting down, was it something specific that kept you coming back to it?
Hmm, I don't think I've tried those before. How do they work?
It takes a lot of courage to consider new options, and it's great that you're open to learning more. What's holding you back from trying something new - is it fear of side effects or something else?
Okay, that sounds like it could be helpful.

You seem to be considering nicotine replacement therapy or prescription medications, which is a great start. What is it about giving up cigarettes that matters most to you right now - is it your health, your family, or something else?
Yeah, that would be great.

It sounds like you're open to learning more about managing weight gain. What's holding you back from taking the first step towards quitting smoking - is there one thing that's keeping you from making a plan to quit?
I suppose I could try to cut down to just one cigarette break a day instead of two or three. That might be a start.

That's a great first step, cutting down to one cigarette break a day - it shows you're willing to make some changes. What's really driving you to make this change, is it more about being healthy, or is there something else that's motivating you to quit smoking?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think I need all the help I can get right now.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit overwhelmed and in need of support, which is completely understandable. Can you tell me what's driving your desire to quit smoking now - was it a recent conversation with a doctor, a family member's concern, or something else entirely?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit a few times, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself. I feel like I should be able to quit, but it's just so hard.

It sounds like you're really struggling with feeling frustrated with yourself when you slip up. Have you noticed if there's a particular moment or feeling that usually leads you to go back to smoking when you're trying to quit?

It's just...I have depression and anxiety, and smoking seems to help me cope with those feelings. But at the same time, I know it's making things worse. And I've tried to quit before, but I always gain weight, and that just makes me feel even more miserable. And my friends and family all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation. And to top it all off, my partner doesn't support me in my quit attempts, so I feel like I'm doing it all on my own.

It sounds like you're feeling really overwhelmed by the combination of your mental health struggles and smoking. Have you ever thought about writing down why quitting matters to you, and what you hope to gain by stopping smoking?

Well, when I'm feeling anxious or overwhelmed, smoking just seems to calm me down, you know? It's like a temporary escape from my emotions. But I know it's not a healthy way to deal with things, and it's just making everything worse in the long run.

It sounds like you've associated smoking with a sense of calm, which is helpful in managing anxiety and overwhelm. Can you imagine taking a short, gentle walk outside instead of reaching for a cigarette when you're feeling anxious?
Yeah, I guess I never thought about that. I've always just focused on quitting smoking, but maybe I need to focus on dealing with the underlying issues first. That makes sense.

It sounds like you're starting to see that quitting smoking might be connected to dealing with your depression and anxiety. One thing you might consider is keeping a journal to track when you feel the urge to smoke and what emotions you're experiencing at those times.

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I mean, I know smoking's bad for me and all, but I've been doing it for years and I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Plus, I've got a lot of other stuff going on in my life right now.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about quitting, Alex. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
I guess I just feel like it's not good for me, and I should probably stop. But like I said, I've got a lot of other stuff going on, and I'm not sure I'm ready to take on quitting smoking too.
It sounds like you've been smoking for a while and now you're considering quitting due to health concerns. How long have you been smoking every day?
Yeah, I've heard that before, but I'm not sure I believe it. I mean, I've smoked for years and I'm fine.
Alex, I can understand your skepticism, but let me ask you, if you were to imagine a day without cigarettes, what would be a small, manageable change you could make to start moving towards that goal?
Yeah, I guess so. I'm worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.

You're worried that quitting smoking might worsen your depression and anxiety. Have you noticed any changes in your depression and anxiety symptoms when you've gone a few days without smoking in the past?
Okay, that sounds like it might be helpful.
Now that we've discussed how quitting smoking might affect your depression and anxiety, I'd like to talk about something that can help you cope with stress, which is a big trigger for you, Alex.
Yeah, I knew that. But like I said, I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit.
You've mentioned weight gain as a concern, Alex. Have you ever tried keeping track of your daily activities and hunger levels to better manage your eating habits when cravings strike?
Okay, I think I'm willing to listen and learn more.
So you're open to learning more strategies to help you quit, Alex. Have you ever tried talking to a friend or family member about your cravings when you were trying to quit in the past?

Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.

I can understand why you'd feel frustrated, given how hard it's been for you to quit in the past. What was going through your mind when you decided to quit each time, and what made you give up eventually?

My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.
I understand how much your daughter's growing up is motivating you to quit. What are some specific moments or situations when you find yourself reaching for a cigarette, maybe around your daughter?

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.

It sounds like having people around you who smoke can make it harder for you to quit. How do you think you would feel if you were surrounded by people who didn't smoke, or if your husband didn't smoke at all?
Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.

It sounds like those online resources aren't really meeting your needs for connection and personalized advice. What do you think is missing for you in those online communities that would make them more helpful?
Cravings. I get these intense cravings, especially during stressful work meetings or when I'm driving. And sometimes I just give in to them.

You've mentioned that cravings are one of your biggest challenges, especially during stressful work meetings or when driving. Have you ever tried changing your environment when you felt a craving coming on, like stepping outside for some fresh air?
Yeah, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to quit for good this time.
You've taken a big step forward – I'm here to support you every step of the way.

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.

I can sense your frustration and disappointment. It's normal to feel stuck, especially when we've experienced setbacks like this before. Can you tell me what motivated you to quit smoking for those two years in the first place?

Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

It sounds like socializing with friends, especially when drinking, has become deeply tied to your smoking habit. Can you tell me how you think your friends feel about your smoking - do they encourage you to quit, or do they seem to be okay with you smoking?

Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

It sounds like you're worried that quitting smoking will make social situations with your friends more awkward. Can you imagine yourself saying "no" to a cigarette offer from a friend, and what words you might use to explain your decision?

I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?

I can sense your frustration and sense of failure. It's understandable, given you were able to quit for 2 years before, and it's clear that experience taught you some valuable lessons. Can you tell me more about what worked for you during those 2 years, and how you can apply those strategies to this current challenge?

Look, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've been smoking for 25 years, and it's not like it's killing me or anything. I enjoy it, it helps me relax, and it's just part of who I am.

It sounds like you've been smoking for a long time and it's become a big part of your daily life, helping you to relax, and you're not sure why you'd want to give it up. What is it that's made you consider quitting now, out of all the years you've been smoking?

Well, like when I'm dealing with a difficult project or client, it calms me down. And when I'm on a break at work or waiting for a job to start, I just smoke out of habit, I guess. And, you know, when I'm with friends or at parties, it makes me feel more relaxed and social. But, honestly, I don't think I can quit. I've tried before, and it didn't work. I don't think I can do it.

Alex, it sounds like cigarettes have become a sort of comfort for you, especially in high-stress situations or when you're feeling bored or social. What is it about trying to quit before that didn't work out for you - was it the physical withdrawal symptoms, or more about feeling like you couldn't replace the habit with something else?

Oh, I don't know... I guess I'd have more money, and my wife would be off my back about it. But, to be honest, I don't think it would make a huge difference. I've got other issues, like my depression and anxiety, that are way more important to deal with. And, like I said, smoking helps me cope with those things. Plus, I don't think I can quit without quitting drinking and pot too, and I'm not willing to do that.

Alex, I hear that you're feeling unsure about quitting smoking because you think it won't make a huge difference in your life, especially with your depression and anxiety being such a big deal for you. It sounds like smoking is closely tied to how you manage those feelings - can you tell me more about what you imagine will happen to those feelings if you were to quit smoking?
I don't know... I'm not sure I believe that. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to talk about it. But, don't expect me to quit anytime soon.

I hear your skepticism, Alex, and I'm not expecting you to quit anytime soon either - we're just exploring the idea. What do you think is the biggest obstacle that's holding you back from even considering quitting smoking?

Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been trying to quit for a while now, but it's just so hard. I've tried cold turkey, nicotine gum, and even prescription medication, but nothing seems to stick. I'm worried that I'll never be able to quit.

You're feeling really overwhelmed, and it's normal to feel that way when we've tried so many things before. Can you tell me what was the one thing that made you try to quit most recently - was it a doctor's advice, a personal reason, or something else?

Well, I know it's bad for my health, and I'm worried about the impact it's having on my lungs and heart. But to be honest, it's also the stress and anxiety that comes with it. I use tobacco as a way to cope with my emotions, and I'm not sure what I'll do without it. I'm afraid that quitting will just make things worse.

It sounds like you're using tobacco as a way to manage your stress and anxiety, Rachel. Have you ever tried taking a few minutes each day to practice deep breathing exercises, where you focus solely on your breath and calm your body?

Honestly, I'm struggling. I quit about a month ago, but it's been tough. I've been using nicotine gum, but I still find myself thinking about smoking all the time. And to be honest, I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always had a hard time with my weight, and I'm scared that quitting smoking will make it worse.

It sounds like you're really struggling with the thought of quitting, and the fear of gaining weight is weighing heavily on you. What is it about not gaining weight that's so important to you, and what do you think would happen if you did gain a few pounds?

I guess I just got tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I was smoking 15-20 a day, and it was taking a toll on my health and my wallet. And to be honest, I was getting tired of the smell and the way it made me feel. But it's hard because I really enjoy smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, and I miss the ritual of it. It's hard to imagine relaxing without a cigarette.

I can sense your frustration with feeling like a slave to cigarettes. It's understandable that you miss the social and ritual aspects of smoking. Considering your struggles with social situations and habit, would you be open to trying a technique that involves redirecting your attention to other people or activities when you're in social situations that trigger cravings?

I think it would be amazing. I would feel so much healthier, and I would be proud of myself for overcoming this addiction. But I'm scared that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse. I've struggled with those conditions for a while, and I'm worried that quitting smoking will make them harder to manage.

It sounds like you're worried that quitting smoking could trigger or worsen your depression and anxiety. Have you noticed any patterns or connections between smoking and how you feel when you're experiencing anxiety or depression?
Yeah, I think that would be helpful. I just feel like I'm stuck and I don't know how to move forward.

You mentioned feeling stuck and unsure about how to move forward. Have you ever thought about planning ahead for situations where you know you're more likely to want to smoke, like social events or parties, and coming up with a strategy to handle them?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two weeks six months ago, but I ended up starting again. It's just so hard. I feel like I've failed.

It sounds like you're really beating yourself up over that setback, but I want you to know that trying to quit and then starting again is actually a normal part of the process for many people. What do you think was one tiny thing that went right for you when you quit for two weeks that first time?

Yeah... I went out with friends to a bar, and we were all having drinks and laughing... and someone offered me a cigarette. I didn't even think twice about it. It was like my brain just shut off, and I was back to my old habits. And then, of course, one cigarette turned into a pack, and now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day.
It sounds like social situations and pressure from others can be particularly challenging for you. What is it about these situations that makes it so hard for you to stick to your decision to quit?

I know it's bad for me, and I hate the way it makes me feel... guilty, anxious, and out of control. But at the same time, I really enjoy smoking. It's hard to explain, but it's just something that makes me feel relaxed and happy. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation when we're together.

It sounds like you have a deep connection to smoking, and it's tied to both the negative feelings it causes and the positive feelings it brings. Can you think of a time when you tried quitting and didn't feel the usual urge to smoke, or a time when you had a moment of clarity that made you realize you wanted to quit for good?
I guess so... I just don't know if I can do it. I feel like I've tried everything already.

I can sense your skepticism and frustration, it's understandable to feel that way after trying and failing before. What is it about quitting that you're holding onto, even though it feels like you've tried everything?
Yeah, okay. I guess that sounds like a good place to start.

You're feeling more open to exploring ways to quit, which is a great step forward. What do you think is the main reason you want to quit this time around - is it really the health concerns, or is there something else that's driving your motivation?
Look, I know I smoke a lot. It's just something I do. I don't really see the big deal. My friends and family all smoke, so it's just a normal part of my life.
It sounds like smoking is pretty ingrained in your daily routine and social life. What are some situations where you find it's really hard to resist the urge to smoke?

It's just something I've always done. I started in college, and it's just become a habit. I don't really think about it that much. And like I said, all my friends do it, so it's not like I'm going to quit just because you're telling me to.
It sounds like you've been smoking for a long time and it's become a deeply ingrained habit. Can you tell me more about what happens in your daily routine that makes you reach for a cigarette?

You don't understand. If I quit, my friends will think I'm lame or something. They'll stop inviting me to hang out. And what am I supposed to do instead of smoking when we're out at the bar? Just stand there and do nothing?

Alex, it sounds like you're worried about how your friends will react and feeling a little lost about what to do in social situations without smoking. Can you tell me more about what you value in your relationships with your friends - is it the smoking part that's so important to you, or is it just being with them?

I guess not, really. I mean, I've tried to quit a couple of times before, but it didn't last. And yeah, it would be nice not to have to worry about finding a place to smoke or having to leave the bar to go outside. But I just don't know if it's worth it.
I understand that you're unsure if quitting is worth it, Alex. Can you tell me what makes you think it's not worth it, specifically?
I guess so. But I'm not promising anything.
It sounds like you're still on the fence about quitting, Alex. Would you be willing to try something small, like telling a friend about your struggles with smoking and asking for their support?

Honestly, it's been tough. I quit a few weeks ago, but I'm still struggling. The anxiety and irritability are killing me. I feel like I'm on edge all the time. And working at the pub doesn't help. Everyone's always smoking and drinking, and it's hard not to join in.
It sounds really tough, especially with the pressure from your work environment. Can you tell me more about what's keeping you from going back to smoking right now, even though it's hard?

The hardest part is dealing with my emotions. I feel like I'm a different person when I'm not smoking. I'm short-tempered, and I just want to lash out at people. And the cravings are constant. I'll be in the middle of a shift, and suddenly I'll smell smoke and it's like my brain is screaming at me to go grab a cigarette.

You're feeling really overwhelmed by your emotions and the cravings, especially when you smell smoke. Can you tell me how much you value quitting for yourself and what would happen if you were able to stay smoke-free?

Yeah, I've tried some stuff. Deep breathing, meditation... but it's hard to do that when you're in the middle of a busy shift. And to be honest, I'm not sure I believe in that stuff. I just feel like I need a cigarette to calm down.
I can see how overwhelming it must be to try to manage your emotions and cravings in the midst of a busy shift. What do you think would need to change for you to feel confident in quitting for good?
Hmm, that's a good point. If I'm not drinking, I might not be as tempted to smoke. But it's hard for me to imagine not having a drink after work. It's just what I do.

It sounds like not having a drink after work might actually reduce your urge to smoke, but it's hard for you to imagine giving up that routine. What's the most appealing thing about having a drink after work - is it the taste, the social aspect, or something else?
Yeah, I think I could do that. One shift a week, that's doable.

I'm glad to hear that committing to not drinking for one shift a week feels like a achievable step for you. What are some things that come to mind when you think about quitting smoking - is it the health benefits, the financial savings, or something else entirely that's driving your desire to quit?
Yeah, that would be great. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I'm not alone in this anymore.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit more hopeful about quitting, and that means a lot to me. What are the biggest rewards you imagine for yourself if you were to quit smoking, and how would that change your life?

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for a few months, but I started again. It's just so hard. I enjoy smoking, you know? It's one of the few things that helps me relax and takes my mind off things.

I can see how quitting must be really tough for you, especially when you enjoy smoking as a way to relax. How did you feel when you quit for those few months - was there something specific that happened or changed for you during that time that might have helped you stay on track, even if only for a little while?
